video_title: America's 250-Year Reset feat. Cem Karsan | Systematic Investor | Ep. 407
gist: Cem Karsan argues the US is entering a multi-decade 'socialism/communism with American characteristics' regime shift: monetizing debt by printing money to fund a $10-15T sovereign wealth fund that buys equities, backstopping a market 'too big to fail', competing with China for dollar dominance, and short-circuiting populism via forced state ownership. He frames positioning/reflexivity as the dominant market driver, expects a calm-then-crisis path around the November midterms into June, and sees the AI-capex boom as an implicitly government-guaranteed bubble.

insights.claim: Karsan argues positioning is the single most reliable driver of market outcomes: when the world is long, odds strongly favor a decline, and when the world is short, odds favor a rally, an effect amplified because a 20% two-month move now represents ~$50 trillion of collateral.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Fade consensus positioning rather than narratives; size the reflexive collateral effect of large index moves when assessing follow-through.
insights.quote: Nothing, literally nothing, is more important than positioning itself. It is the only thing that you can rely on reliably to have a meaningful effect on the outcome.

insights.claim: The failure of oil to reach the widely predicted $150-$200 during the Iran conflict is cited as proof that markets rapidly strip out risk premia once crises clear, because everyone was already positioned for the 'obvious and inevitable' outcome.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Consensus 'inevitable' macro trades are usually already priced; the pain trade is the opposite move.
insights.quote: everybody thought they were positioned for something that was obvious and inevitable. So, once again, the opposite happened.

insights.claim: With oil shorts now at record highs after bulls capitulated, Karsan flags oil long as a newly interesting contrarian bet precisely because positioning has flipped to one extreme.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Record short positioning in oil sets up asymmetric upside; watch for a squeeze once the crowd is fully offside.
insights.quote: now everybody seems to have thrown in the towel and uh we have oil shorts at record highs now

insights.claim: The 'summer of George' describes a seasonal regime where summer volume halves while structured-product supply stays constant, driving volatility compression at the index level and forcing massive correlation breakdowns and single-name dispersion.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: In summer, favor dispersion/relative-value trades over index direction; long what's shorted, short what's crowded-long.
insights.quote: if you have all compression at the index level, that tends to mean if something goes up, something else has to go down. And uh because the net has to be zero and so you get massive correlation breakdowns in the summer.

themes.theme: US Sovereign Wealth Fund & Debt Monetization

insights.claim: Karsan predicts the US will create a $10-15 trillion sovereign wealth fund over the next decade that buys equities financed by printing money, backstopping the Treasury market and monetizing the debt.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: A structural state bid under US equities and long-end Treasuries argues for owning equities and duration into the buildout, and for structurally higher inflation.
insights.quote: They are going to backs stop the Treasury market and they are going to create a sovereign wealth fund that buys within the next decade 10 to 15 trillion dollars of equities

insights.claim: Karsan frames the emerging US model as 'socialism/communism with American characteristics' where the government buys corporate stakes on behalf of the people through the free market to short-circuit populism, mirroring how China used a sovereign wealth fund and direct investment for a decade.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: State capitalism means government picks winners/losers; strategically favored sectors (AI, semis, defense) get a policy tailwind.
insights.quote: You are buying corporations on behalf of the people through the air quote free market system.

insights.claim: Karsan identifies five accelerating pressures forcing this regime change: unsustainable debt, populism, structural inflation, a financial market too big to fail, and an intractable conflict with China over reserve-currency dominance.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: These five vectors form the core macro thesis; each is a lens for positioning over the coming decade.
insights.quote: It's not just that they exist. It's that they are and that they're increasing. It's that they are accelerating as well.

insights.claim: The US government has already taken five corporate stakes (including ~10% of Intel and a proposed 5% of OpenAI) and Karsan expects this to escalate to over a trillion dollars of stakes in a broad swath of businesses, likely trillions within a year or two.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Watch for accelerating government equity stakes as a signal the plan is live; targeted names get repriced up on the windfall.
insights.quote: This is the fifth stake they've taken in a company.

insights.claim: Karsan cites the Intel stake as evidence of the mechanism: the stock jumped ~40% after the ~10% government stake, delivering a windfall to the Treasury coffers while giving Washington leverage to push a 'hire American' mandate.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Government-stake announcements can be re-rating catalysts; the fiscal windfall funds social spending, reinforcing the reflexive loop.
insights.quote: Intel was trading when they took a 10% stake trading what up 40

insights.claim: Karsan argues the US can print inordinate amounts of money to fund this without becoming Zimbabwe or Turkey because it uniquely retains natural structural demand for the dollar as the reserve currency.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: The dollar's reserve status is the enabling leverage; its erosion is the key risk that could break the whole plan.
insights.quote: printing money cannot happen in England or Turkey or Zimbabwe because they don't have natural structural demand for the currency

insights.claim: Karsan explicitly states the point of the plan is to drive massive inflation, because monetizing the debt away is the only path to reduce it.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Position for structurally higher inflation and negative real rates; favor real assets and equities over long nominal bonds over the long run.
insights.quote: yes, you drive massive inflation, but that's kind of the point because the only way you get rid of the debt is through monetizing it anyway.

insights.claim: Karsan contends earnings growth is now 100% coming from AI capex buildout in a reflexive loop, citing that ~60% of Microsoft's earnings last quarter came from the increased value of its OpenAI stake rather than underlying demand.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Headline earnings growth is capex/mark-to-market driven, not demand driven; a capex pause would expose the broader 'meh' economy.
insights.quote: Microsoft showing 60 I think 60% of its earnings last quarter was from the increased value of anthropic. It's not earnings because of demand

insights.claim: Karsan argues this AI capex cycle differs from the dot-com bubble because there is an implicit government guarantee: Washington will buy stock and bail out the hyperscalers, so executives like Larry Ellison and Zuckerberg mortgage everything to spend at scale.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: An implicit policy backstop means the AI capex bubble can inflate far longer than valuations suggest; don't short it while the state is bidding.
insights.quote: capex is different this time because there's an implicit guarantee the US government's going to keep driving that cap.

insights.claim: Karsan cites an alleged hot-mic moment where Zuckerberg told Trump 'Sorry, boss. I didn't know what number you actually wanted me to say in terms of total capex' as evidence of an explicit wink-wink arrangement on capex targets.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: If capex figures are politically coordinated, they reflect policy goals more than genuine demand, changing how to read AI-spend guidance.
insights.quote: Sorry, boss. I I didn't uh I didn't know what number you actually wanted me to say in terms of total capex.

insights.claim: Karsan characterizes the arrangement as a 'protection racket': AI firms that don't play along with government capex goals risk the state declining to buy their stock or support them.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Corporate alignment with policy becomes a survival factor; watch which firms get government stakes vs. left out.
insights.quote: this kind of how Trump operates a protection racket you don't you don't do it you don't play along

insights.claim: Karsan states the market is definitively in a bubble that goes 'very badly' without the continued investment, promises and control of the Federal Reserve and Treasury, making state control the load-bearing assumption of the bull case.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: The bubble's survival hinges on whether the state retains control; a loss of control is the tail risk to hedge.
insights.quote: we are definitely in a bubble

insights.claim: Karsan argues US actions in Iran, Venezuela, Panama and the Philippines are all about building a moat for the dollar by ensuring commodities and trade continue to be settled in dollars, not about nuclear weapons.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Read geopolitical flashpoints as dollar-defense moves; watch the Strait of Hormuz and yuan-denominated trade as the real battleground.
insights.quote: They are creating a moat for the US dollar.

insights.claim: Karsan predicts the Iran conflict is not ending and expects 'full throttle re-engagement' in the November-to-June window, arguing the current deal is only a temporary pause tied to the midterm election.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Geopolitical risk premium in oil/energy is likely to re-emerge post-midterms; the calm is election-driven, not structural.
insights.quote: if I was going to play this game quote unquote Donald Trump um I would wait till the midterms then I would attack Iran.

insights.claim: Karsan says the US will not tolerate China or Iran gaining any leverage or taxing power over the Strait of Hormuz, viewing control of that trade route as a non-negotiable breaking point for dollar-denominated commodity trade.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Hormuz control is a hard red line; escalation risk there is underpriced given US strategic stakes.
insights.quote: they want control of the street uh at least uh by proxy

insights.claim: Karsan suggests part of the US-China accommodation may be allowing China to buy into more US businesses as active owners, provided there is no operational influence.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: A negotiated cross-ownership truce could ease tail risk; the 'no influence' line is the constraint to monitor.

insights.claim: Karsan reads Kevin Warsh and Scott Bessent as a coordinated good-cop/bad-cop pair, arguing Fed 'independence' is shrinking and that Warsh will merely appear independent to preserve dollar credibility while the plan is set by the administration and Treasury.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Discount Fed 'independence' signaling; a coordinated Fed-Treasury implies deliberate curve control and easier policy than data would warrant.
insights.quote: so Borch is a good cop that's all he is

insights.claim: Karsan lays out the sequencing: Treasury (Bessent) will buy back its own debt first, and then into a crisis the Federal Reserve will step in to backstop the long end of the curve.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Expect Treasury buybacks as an early tell, with Fed long-end YCC-style intervention arriving on the next crisis; position duration accordingly.
insights.quote: the Treasury will buy back its own debt first and then into crisis the Federal Reserve will hop on and begin to backs stop the long end of the curve.

insights.claim: Karsan notes it is not a coincidence that two hedge fund managers head the Treasury and the Fed for the first time, given a market-focused strategic plan is about to be deployed for markets 'too big to fail'.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Personnel signals intent; market-savvy leadership implies more active market management than traditional central banking.
insights.quote: not a coincidence that we have two hedge fund managers at the for the first time

insights.claim: Karsan's short-to-medium-term market framework rests on three legs: structural flows (seasonal/positioning), macro (rates, inflation, growth), and the activist administration itself, which now matters more than ever in a populist regime.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Score the three legs to time risk; when all three align negative (post-midterm), reduce exposure.
insights.quote: There's three majors. There's flows... Two, there's macro... the third stool... is the administration itself.

insights.claim: Karsan expects a benign, buy-the-dip market until roughly September (flows positive, administration supportive, macro only slowly degrading), advising against fighting the administration during this window.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Stay constructive/long into September; the flows and policy backdrop favor upside despite deteriorating macro.
insights.quote: for now until September or so, right, it could be choppy, but you know, don't expect any negative fireworks

insights.claim: Karsan flags November to June as the dangerous window when all three legs turn: flows fade after mid-December, macro worsens, and an administration that has secured the midterms may welcome a controlled crisis to reset the market before the presidential cycle.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Target November-June for hedges and reduced risk; a deliberate 'take it down to take it up' reset is the base case.
insights.quote: you have an administration that probably wants a bit of a crisis, a controlled crisis, but a crisis. They don't want to lose control.

insights.claim: Karsan notes midterm election years are historically the worst for equities, with Q2 and Q3 typically the weakest, and says the administration has been unusually proactive in stabilizing the macro to counter this.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Seasonal midterm weakness argues for caution in Q2-Q3, but proactive policy support can override the historical pattern near-term.
insights.quote: midterm years like which is this year are historically the worst

insights.claim: Karsan argues this midterm matters more than usual because if Trump loses both houses he loses the control needed to deploy the plan, so the administration will do anything to at least win the Senate.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Policy will be maximally market-supportive into the midterms; a Senate loss is the scenario that unlocks downside via loss of control.
insights.quote: Trump is at risk if he loses both houses of losing control at a time where he needs control, if he wants to deploy the plan.

insights.claim: Karsan says call-vol (right-tail upside volatility) is inordinately cheap, as bigger and faster upside moves increasingly pay off, especially in high-dispersion pockets like AI hyperscalers that ran ~50% in two months.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Own upside convexity/call-vol in dispersed high-beta names rather than left-tail hedges; the right tail is where the payoff is.
insights.quote: And callvall is inordinately cheap. We've highlighted this now for a while. Um, you know, you're getting bigger and bigger fast moves to the upside.

insights.claim: Karsan explains that traditional long-vol equity hedges delivered roughly zero this year despite a ~9-10% drawdown into March, because the decline was too slow to monetize before a very fast rally erased it, echoing 2022 and April 2025.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Slow grinding declines defeat long-vol hedges; hedgers need convex or path-aware structures, not naive long puts.
insights.quote: the move was so slow and into that 9 10% decline that we had into March that there was no real money to be made from a longfall trade

insights.claim: Trend-followers posted a solid first half of 2026 despite a challenging June, with the SG Trend index down 1.29% in June but still up ~9% YTD, currencies the main bright spot amid sharp reversals in silver, gold and base metals.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: June commodity reversals hurt CTAs but the trend book remains net positive YTD; currencies and rates carried performance.
insights.quote: Stocken trend down 1.29. 29% still up 9% for the year.

insights.claim: Host reports the S&P 500 was down just under 1% in June but up 10.21% YTD, while world government bonds were down 0.37% YTD, underscoring equity strength versus weak fixed income in 2026.
insights.speaker: Niels Kaastrup-Larsen (host)
insights.so_what: Equities materially outpacing bonds YTD is consistent with the inflation/monetization thesis favoring stocks over duration.

insights.claim: Karsan attributes soft labor numbers to a K-shaped economy where capex, not labor, drives everything, and AI-infrastructure capex is not labor-intensive, leaving the rest of the economy 'meh'.
insights.speaker: Cem Karsan (guest)
insights.so_what: Weak jobs data need not derail markets since earnings ride capex, not employment; the divide fuels the populism driving the whole regime shift.
insights.quote: capex is driving everything but it's capex um that's not a very laborheavy capex
